articles.title: The Great Stork Derby: When a Millionaire's Prank Turned Babies Into a Competition
articles.summary: What if a millionaire left his fortune to the woman who had the most babies? That's exactly what happened in Canada during the Great Depression. Charles Vance Millar, a wealthy Toronto lawyer and prankster, died in 1926. His will included a bizarre clause: the mother who gave birth to the most children in 10 years would win a huge prize — worth nearly 9 million Canadian dollars today. The contest, called the Great Stork Derby, sparked legal battles, media frenzy, and intense public debate about morality, class, and immigration. Two leading contenders, Grace Bagnato (an Italian immigrant) and Florence Brown (a fifth-generation Canadian), were pitted against each other in the press. Brown even said she didn't want an Italian to…

articles.title: Alcaraz Out of French Open: Wrist Injury Sidelines Two-Time Champion
articles.summary: Two-time defending French Open champion Carlos Alcaraz has withdrawn from this year's tournament due to a right wrist injury. The 22-year-old Spanish tennis star hurt his wrist during his first match at the Barcelona Open and subsequently pulled out of the Italian Open and Roland Garros. Alcaraz, who won the Australian Open earlier this year, is the youngest man to complete a career Grand Slam. His absence opens the door for world number one Jannik Sinner, who has never won the French Open. Alcaraz said on Instagram that he needs to be cautious and hopes to return for Wimbledon.

articles.title: Before Apps, There Were Green Stamps: The History of Customer Loyalty
articles.summary: Long before smartphone apps and digital points, there were Green Stamps. In 1896, the Sperry & Hutchinson Company (S&H) launched a loyalty program where shoppers received small green stamps for every 10 cents spent. Filled booklets could be redeemed for merchandise like lamps or bicycles. By the 1960s, S&H was distributing 32 million catalogs a year and had 800 redemption centers across the US. Andy Warhol even immortalized the stamps in a silkscreen. But the program declined in the 1970s as the economy stagnated and retailers launched their own rewards. Today, loyalty programs account for $12 billion in economic activity, yet 26% of points in the US go unused — worth $10 billion annually.
